q: The term ‘Biodiversity’ was coined by Edward O. Wilson in 1985.
Biodiversity can be defined as the totality of genes, species and ecosystem of a region.
Biodiversity plays a significant role in maintaining the balance of the ecosystem.
Ecosystem diversity is usually taken to include diversity at three levels: variation in ecosystems, species number, and genetic variation.
An ecosystem is a community of organisms and their physical environment interacting each other’s together.
The diversity of ecological complexity includes variations in ecological niches, trophic structure, food-webs and nutrient cycling.
Species richness only considers the measure of the evenness of the abundance of the different species.
The more species there are, and the more evenly the numbers of organisms are distributed among the different species, the greater the species diversity.
Ecosystems with high species diversity tend to be more stable than ones with limited diversity because they are more able to resist changes.
Genetic diversity refers to the variety of genetic information contained in all of the individual plants, animals, fungi and microorganisms.
The genetic diversity that exists in natural populations is always obvious.
Genetic differences between populations of the same species exist because populations may be adapted slightly differently in different parts of their ranges.
Genetic diversity is important in providing populations with the ability to adapt to changes in temperature, salinity, humidity and rainfall.
Large-scale ecosystems can function and self-regulate only when biodiversity is reduced.
If biodiversity is reduced in one area, the natural balance may be destroyed elsewhere.
Each species in an ecosystem has a specific niche which is a role to play.
Rich biodiversity will support a larger number of plant species and, therefore, a greater variety of crops.
Healthy ecosystems aid in breaking down pollutants.
Healthy ecosystems and rich biodiversity provide nutrient storage and recycling.
Rich biodiversity contributes to climate stability.
Healthy ecosystems slow down the recovery from natural disasters.
Biodiversity is very important for food security, economy and livelihoods.
All organisms in an ecosystem live in interdependence sharing living space, shelter and available food.
The term ‘Biodiversity’ refers to the variety and variability among living organisms and the ecological complexes in which they occur.
Myanmar is the largest country in mainland Southeast Asia with a land area of 676,577 $km^{2}$.
Myanmar's large latitudinal range of 2,100 km contributes to its diversity of topography and habitats.
The diverse habitats of Myanmar are home for a narrow range of species.
Forest ecosystems are a carbon sink and stabilize soils.
By slowing water flow due to increased infiltration, forests help regulate the seasonal flow of water downstream.
Forests support base flow in streams by recharging sources of groundwater.
Forests help to maintain the functioning of dam and reservoir infrastructure by impeding the loss of soils.
Forest constitutes the dominant vegetation type in Myanmar.
The total area of forest in Myanmar is 290,410 $km^{2}$, which is 50% of the total land area.
Only half of Myanmar's forest is described as closed forest.
Rainfall and elevation strongly influence the distribution of different vegetation types in Myanmar.
Dry forest occurs where rainfall is usually less than 400 mm a year and support xerophytic types of vegetation.
Mixed Deciduous Forest accounts for 38% of Forest Areas in Myanmar.
Fallow Land accounts for 10% of forest areas in Myanmar.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources (IUCN) publishes the Red Data Book.
The Red Data Book includes the list of only animal species that are extinct.
The red data symbolizes the warning signal for those species which are endangered.
Myanmar is a moderately biodiverse country with about 250 mammal species.
More than 1,000 birds are recorded in Myanmar.
Among species in Myanmar, 39 species of mammals and 38 species of plants are globally threatened.
The scientific name of Kanyin is *Dipterocarpus alatus*.
Mahogany has the scientific name *Swietenia macrophylla* and its IUCN status is Endangered.
Kyauk Htinn shuu has the common name Mann’s plum yew and its scientific name is *Cephalotaxus mannii*.
The Black Orchid (*Paphiopedilum wardii*) is listed as Endangered by the IUCN.
The scientific name of Taung Zalat Ni is *Rhododendron arboreum*.
The Asian Elephant (*Elephas maximus*) is classified as Critically Endangered by the IUCN.
The scientific name for Eld’s Deer is *Rucervus eldii thamin*.
Irrawaddy Dolphin has the scientific name *Orcaella brevirostris*.
Green Peafowl (*Pavo muticus*) is known in Myanmar as Daun.
The Burmese Star Tortoise (*Geochelone platynota*) has the IUCN status of Critically Endangered.
More than 3 billion people depend on marine and coastal biodiversity for their livelihoods.
The loss of biodiversity affects the lives of more than 1.6 billion people living in dry lands.
Ecosystems and species are being lost only by the direct action of humans.
Habitat loss and the degradation of the environment is one of the five major threats to biodiversity.
The effects of invasive alien species on native species is a major threat to biodiversity.
Habitat destruction leads to habitat loss which has played a key role in extinction.
Habitat fragmentation occurs when habitats become divided into small areas.
Physically larger species and those living at higher latitudes are more sensitive to reduction in habitat area.
Dam construction and encroaching are major reasons for habitat destruction.
Most cultivation in Myanmar employs techniques that cannot degrade the natural environment.
Shifting cultivation in upland areas and gold mining are examples of land-use change that degrade the environment.
Habitat loss is the most significant cause of biodiversity loss globally.
The replacement of local landraces with modern varieties is a threat to agrobiodiversity.
Urbanization and overgrazing are major threats to the agrobiodiversity of Myanmar.
Pollution is a growing threat only in aquatic ecosystems.
Invasive alien species (IAS) can be introduced unintentionally by tourists or through the transport of cargo.
Water-hyacinth is an invasive alien plant species in Myanmar.
Genetic pollution indicates the loss of identity of wild plant species as a result of transfer of genes from crop plants.
Significant reproductive barriers exist between most wild species and crop plants.
Gene flow is expected to have its greatest effect on weed species that are closely related to crop plants.
It is easily possible to recall genetically engineered organisms back to the laboratory once released into the field.
Wind, rain, and insect pollinators can carry genetically altered pollen into adjoining fields.
Overexploitation refers to the destruction of a resource because it will be unable to replenish.
Overfishing is the removal of a species of fish at a rate greater than the species can replenish its population naturally.
Overlogging is a form of overexploitation caused only by illegal logging activities.
Overhunting threatens one-third of endangered vertebrates.
Bushmeat provides increased opportunity for transmission of several zoonotic viruses from animal hosts to humans.
Rising ocean temperatures and diminishing Arctic sea ice affects marine biodiversity.
Human overpopulation leads to additional strains on resources and spikes in pollution.
Wildlife trade refers only to the commerce of live animals.
Illegal wildlife trade contributes directly to the loss of species and habitat destruction.
Smuggled wildlife is an increasing global demand for exotic pets, food, and jewelry.
Pangolins are the only scaly mammal on earth and account for as much as 20% of all illegal wildlife trade.
The primary drivers of the intercontinental pangolin trade are pangolin meat and scales.
Wildlife smuggling affects biodiversity by putting a strain on the environment's resources through interspecific competition.
Outbreaks of zoonotic diseases including COVID-19 and SARS have been linked to live wildlife markets.
Wildlife trade hotspots are places where targeted action for protection is particularly worthwhile.
CITES is an international law for wildlife protection.
Myanmar enacted the Protected Areas Law in 2018.
Conservation means keeping and protecting a living and changing environment.
Active intervention is required to restore areas and to protect native species.
The Broadly Utilitarian reason for conservation states that every species has a right to live.
Narrowly Utilitarian products include food, fibres, drugs and medicines obtained from biodiversity.
Broadly Utilitarian benefits include ecosystem services like providing oxygen and controlling floods.
The Ethical Utilitarian view suggests every living species has an intrinsic value.
There are mainly four conservation methods: in situ, ex situ, in vivo, and ex vivo.
In situ methods are the ways of conservation of species which take place in the natural habitat of the organism.
Maintaining the natural habitat means that all ‘life support systems’ are provided.
Keeping organisms in situ means putting them in the ecosystem where they belong.
Active management of nature reserves involves the continuous monitoring and maintenance of effective boundaries.
Ex situ methods conserve animals and plants within their natural habitats.
Ex situ conservation methods are usually used as a last resort.
The three methods of ex situ conservation are captive breeding, storage of seeds in the seed bank and cultivation in botanical garden.
In captive breeding programmes, individuals of endangered species are bred in zoos and parks.
Artificial insemination and embryo transfer are techniques used in captive breeding.
Cryogenics involves freezing eggs, sperm cells and embryos for future use.
Keeping a pedigree in captive breeding is intended to encourage inbreeding.
Reintroduction programmes can be very expensive and time consuming and they may fail.
It has been estimated that 25% of the world’s flowering plant species could disappear within the next 50 years.
Seeds in a seed bank are dried and then stored at a temperature of $14 ^{\circ}C$.
Seed metabolism slows down in cold, dark conditions, preventing seeds from germinating.
Saved and viable seeds contain a treasure trove of useful genes for developing improved varieties of food crops.
About 80,000 plant species are grown in botanical gardens all over the world.
One of the major objectives of botanical gardens is to create and support collections of native taxa.
In Myanmar, 42 protected areas have been established.
Scientific reserves are held for protection of features for ‘scientific study, research, education, and the benefit of the country’.
Settlement and resource harvesting is allowed in a national park.
A marine national park is a permanent marine reservation for the conservation of species.
Nature reserve is a protected area of importance for flora, fauna, or features of geological or other special interest.
In a wildlife sanctuary, capturing, killing and poaching of animals is strictly prohibited.
Community forest involves sustainable forest management and utilization activities with the local community.
